knowledge.what_it_is: Quality Assurance (QA) is an essential process in software development that ensures products meet requirements and are defect-free, focusing on preventing defects rather than finding them. It involves a systematic approach to identify, document, and report defects, with the goal of improving overall product quality.
what_you_will_learn: Defect Life Cycle — understanding the stages of a defect from identification to closure
Test Case Management — creating, executing, and maintaining test cases using tools like TestRail or PractiTest
Test Data Management — planning, designing, and managing test data for efficient testing
Test Environment Setup — configuring and maintaining test environments for accurate testing
Defect Tracking and Reporting — using tools like JIRA, Trello, or Bugzilla to track and report defects
Risk-Based Testing (RBT) — identifying high-risk areas and prioritizing testing efforts accordingly
Equivalence Partitioning (EP) — dividing input data into partitions for effective testing
Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) — testing values at the boundaries of valid input ranges
subtopics: test automation frameworks like selenium or appium
test data generation tools like testcomplete or ranorex

knowledge.what_it_is: A test oracle is a reference or standard used to determine whether a software application meets its requirements and behaves as expected. In QA, test oracles are critical for ensuring that tests are reliable and effective.
what_you_will_learn: Test Oracles — the importance of having a clear understanding of what constitutes correct behavior in software applications
Requirements-based Testing — using functional requirements to create test cases and evaluate test results
Behavioral Modeling — creating behavioral models to describe expected system behavior and validate against actual behavior
Equivalence Partitioning — dividing input data into partitions based on their equivalence classes to reduce testing effort
Boundary Value Analysis — testing the boundaries of valid input ranges to ensure correct handling of edge cases
State Transition Testing — evaluating how a system transitions between different states in response to user interactions or events
Error Guessing — using domain knowledge and experience to guess potential error scenarios and test for them
Exploratory Testing — using a combination of scripted and unscripted testing techniques to explore the application's behavior

knowledge.what_it_is: A QA mindset involves adopting a systematic and structured approach to quality assurance, focusing on identifying defects early in the development cycle, and ensuring that software meets its intended requirements.
what_you_will_learn: Defect Life Cycle — understanding the stages of defect identification, reporting, and resolution
Test-Driven Development (TDD) — writing automated tests before coding to ensure testability and maintainability
Behavior Driven Development (BDD) — using natural language to describe expected behavior in tests
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines — automating testing, building, and deployment processes
Test Data Management — creating and managing test data to support automated testing
Defect Prioritization — determining the severity and impact of defects for effective prioritization
Risk-Based Testing — identifying high-risk areas and focusing testing efforts on those areas
Exploratory Testing — using a flexible, non-scripted approach to identify unexpected issues

knowledge.what_it_is: Test prioritization is the process of determining which tests to run first, based on risk, business value, and other factors. It's a critical skill for QAs to ensure that high-priority tests are executed efficiently, while also minimizing the time spent on low-priority ones.
what_you_will_learn: Risk-based testing — identifying high-risk areas of the application and prioritizing tests accordingly
Business value-driven testing — focusing on tests that deliver the most business value to the organization
Test case prioritization matrices — using tools like MoSCoW or Kano to prioritize test cases based on their relative importance
Prioritization frameworks — applying frameworks like Prioritization Matrix, RICE (Reach, Impact, Confidence, Effort), or Cost of Delay to guide prioritization decisions
Test data management — ensuring that relevant and accurate test data is available for high-priority tests
Automated testing — leveraging tools like Selenium, Appium, or TestComplete to automate repetitive and low-priority tests
Prioritization in agile development — adapting prioritization strategies to accommodate iterative and incremental development methodologies
Test environment management — ensuring that the right test environments are available for high-priority tests

knowledge.what_it_is: Testing approaches are methods and techniques used by Quality Assurance (QA) professionals to ensure that software applications meet the required quality standards. This includes identifying, analyzing, and prioritizing defects, as well as developing test cases and executing tests to validate the application's functionality.
what_you_will_learn: Black Box Testing — testing an application without knowledge of its internal workings, focusing on inputs and outputs
White Box Testing — testing an application with knowledge of its internal workings, focusing on code coverage and path analysis
Gray Box Testing — a combination of black box and white box testing, where some knowledge of the internal workings is available
Equivalence Partitioning — dividing input data into partitions to identify which inputs are equivalent in terms of functionality
Boundary Value Analysis — testing the boundaries of each partition to ensure that the application behaves as expected
State Transition Testing — testing how an application's state changes in response to different inputs and actions
Use Case Testing — testing an application based on its use cases, which describe how users interact with the system
Exploratory Testing — a flexible approach to testing where the tester explores the application without prior knowledge of its functionality

knowledge.what_it_is: Test management involves planning, organizing, and controlling testing activities to ensure that software meets its requirements and is delivered on time.
what_you_will_learn: Test Planning — creating a comprehensive plan for testing, including test scope, schedule, and resources
Test Case Development — designing and writing detailed test cases to cover all aspects of the software
Test Data Management — creating and managing test data to support testing activities
Defect Tracking and Reporting — using tools like JIRA, Trello, or Bugzilla to track and report defects found during testing
Test Environment Setup — configuring and maintaining a stable and reliable test environment for testing
Risk-Based Testing — identifying high-risk areas of the software and prioritizing testing efforts accordingly
Exploratory Testing — using techniques like session-based testing or risk-based testing to identify unknown issues
Test Automation Frameworks — designing and implementing automation frameworks using tools like Selenium, Appium, or TestComplete

knowledge.what_it_is: Project management in QA involves planning, organizing, and controlling testing activities to ensure timely delivery of high-quality software products.
what_you_will_learn: Agile methodologies — applying Scrum or Kanban principles to manage testing tasks and collaborate with cross-functional teams
Test case management tools — using Atlassian TestRail, PractiTest, or TestLink to create, execute, and report test cases
Defect tracking systems — utilizing JIRA, Bugzilla, or Trello to record, prioritize, and track defects found during testing
Risk-based testing — identifying high-risk areas of the software and prioritizing testing efforts accordingly
Test data management — using tools like Test Data Manager or DBT to manage test data and reduce testing time
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines — integrating automated testing into CI/CD workflows for faster feedback loops
Test automation frameworks — selecting frameworks like Selenium, Appium, or Cypress to automate repetitive testing tasks
Metrics and KPIs in testing — understanding metrics like test coverage, defect density, and lead time to measure testing effectiveness

knowledge.what_it_is: White box testing, also known as glass box testing or clear box testing, is a software testing technique where the tester has access to the internal workings of the code and can test individual components or modules. It involves reviewing code, identifying potential issues, and creating test cases based on that knowledge.
what_you_will_learn: Code Review — understanding how to review code for quality, security, and performance issues
Control Flow Analysis — analyzing the flow of control in a program to identify potential bugs or vulnerabilities
Data Flow Analysis — identifying data dependencies and flows within a program to ensure correctness
Static Code Analysis Tools — using tools like SonarQube, CodeCoverage, or Pylint to analyze code quality and security
Dynamic Code Analysis — using tools like debuggers, profilers, or memory analyzers to understand runtime behavior
API Documentation Review — understanding how to review API documentation for completeness, accuracy, and consistency
Code Smells Identification — identifying code smells such as duplicated code, long methods, or tight coupling
Design Patterns and Principles — understanding design patterns like Singleton, Factory, or Observer, and principles like KISS or YAGNI

knowledge.what_it_is: Gray box testing is an intermediate-level software testing technique that combines elements of black box and white box testing, where the tester has some knowledge of the internal workings of the system but not complete visibility. It's used to identify defects at a higher level than white box testing while still leveraging internal knowledge.
what_you_will_learn: Gray box testing techniques — using a combination of black box and white box approaches, such as equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, and decision table-based testing
System documentation review — understanding system architecture, data flow diagrams, and other relevant documentation to inform test cases
Code reviews — identifying potential defects in code through static analysis tools like SonarQube or CodeCoverage
API testing with Postman or cURL — sending HTTP requests to APIs to validate responses and error handling
Fuzz testing with libraries like American Fuzzy Lop (AFL) or libFuzzer — injecting random input data to identify crashes or unexpected behavior
State transition testing — modeling system states and transitions to ensure correct behavior under various conditions

knowledge.what_it_is: Black box testing is a software testing method where the internal workings of the system are unknown, and only the inputs and outputs are considered. It's an essential technique in QA to ensure that the system behaves as expected without knowing its internal implementation.
what_you_will_learn: Test case design — creating test cases based on functional requirements, user stories, or use cases
Equivalence partitioning — dividing input data into partitions to reduce testing efforts and increase coverage
Boundary value analysis — testing the boundaries of input values to ensure correct behavior
State transition testing — verifying that the system behaves correctly during state transitions
Decision table-based testing — creating test cases based on decision tables to cover all possible combinations of inputs
Error guessing — using experience and knowledge to identify potential errors or defects in the system
Black box vs. white box testing — understanding the differences between these two testing approaches and when to use each
Test data management — creating, storing, and managing test data for efficient testing
subtopics: black box vs. white box testing comparison
test automation frameworks for black box testing

knowledge.what_it_is: Testing techniques are methods and approaches used by Quality Assurance (QA) professionals to ensure that software applications meet the required quality standards, are reliable, and function as expected. These techniques help identify defects early on in the development cycle, reducing the overall cost of testing and improving the overall quality of the product.
what_you_will_learn: Equivalence Partitioning — dividing input data into partitions to ensure that all possible inputs are tested
Boundary Value Analysis — testing values at the boundaries of valid input ranges to ensure correct behavior
State Transition Testing — testing how a system behaves when transitioning between different states or modes
Decision Table Testing — using decision tables to systematically test complex decision-making logic in software
Error Guessing — identifying potential error scenarios and testing for them proactively
Exploratory Testing — testing with minimal planning, focusing on understanding the application's behavior and identifying defects
Pairwise Testing — testing all possible combinations of input parameters to ensure correct behavior
State Machine Testing — modeling a system as a state machine and testing its behavior in different states

knowledge.what_it_is: Functional testing is a type of software testing that ensures an application or system works as expected from a user's perspective, focusing on its functionality and behavior.
what_you_will_learn: Test Scenarios — creating detailed descriptions of expected application behavior for each test case
Test Cases — defining specific inputs, actions, and expected outcomes for functional testing
Test Data Management — strategies for generating, storing, and reusing test data to reduce maintenance efforts
Equivalence Partitioning — dividing input values into partitions to identify valid and invalid test cases
Boundary Value Analysis — testing the boundaries of input values to ensure correct behavior
State Transition Testing — verifying that an application's state changes correctly in response to user interactions
User Story Mapping — creating visual representations of user stories to identify functional requirements
Acceptance Criteria — defining clear, testable conditions for determining whether a feature meets its functional requirements

knowledge.what_it_is: qTest is a comprehensive test management platform used by QA teams to plan, track, and report on software testing activities. It supports Agile and Waterfall methodologies and integrates with popular development tools like JIRA, Trello, and GitHub.
what_you_will_learn: Creating test cases — defining test steps, expected results, and test data in qTest
Assigning test cases to test cycles — planning and prioritizing testing activities
Running automated tests from qTest — integrating with CI/CD pipelines and executing tests using APIs or plugins
Tracking defects and issues — creating, assigning, and tracking defect reports within qTest
Generating test reports — customizing report templates, exporting data to Excel, and sharing results with stakeholders
Configuring test environments — setting up virtual machines, defining environment variables, and managing test data
Integrating with CI/CD tools — automating testing workflows using Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, or Azure DevOps
Using qTest's API — creating custom integrations, automating tasks, and extending qTest's functionality

knowledge.what_it_is: TestRail is a comprehensive test management tool used by QA teams to plan, organize, and track software testing activities. It supports Agile and traditional testing methodologies, integrates with various issue tracking systems, and provides detailed reporting capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Creating test cases — defining clear and concise requirements for each test case
Test runs — organizing and executing multiple test cases in a single run
Test suites — grouping related test cases together to simplify testing and reporting
Prioritization — assigning priority levels to test cases based on business requirements
Defect tracking — linking test results to defects in issue tracking systems like JIRA, Trello, or Asana
Custom fields — creating custom fields to store additional metadata for each test case or run
TestRail API — using the RESTful API to automate testing tasks and integrate with other tools

knowledge.what_it_is: Atlassian is a suite of collaboration and project management tools, widely used by software development teams to track issues, manage workflows, and collaborate on projects. Key products include Jira, Confluence, Bitbucket, and Trello.
what_you_will_learn: Jira workflow management — creating custom workflows for issue tracking and project management
Jira boards and dashboards — configuring and using Kanban boards, Scrum boards, and dashboards to visualize project progress
Confluence page creation and editing — writing and formatting documentation in a collaborative environment
Bitbucket repository management — setting up and managing Git repositories for version control
Trello board configuration — creating and customizing boards, lists, and cards for task management
Jira automation rules — using Jira's built-in automation features to streamline workflows and reduce manual effort
Confluence integration with Jira — linking Confluence pages to Jira issues and projects

knowledge.what_it_is: Assembla is a cloud-based test management tool that helps teams plan, track, and report on software testing efforts. It provides features such as test case management, defect tracking, and reporting to ensure efficient and effective testing.
what_you_will_learn: Test Case Management — creating, editing, and linking test cases with requirements and defects in Assembla
Defect Tracking — identifying, prioritizing, and resolving defects found during testing using Assembla's defect tracking feature
Test Planning — planning and organizing testing efforts using Assembla's test planning features such as test case allocation and execution
Reporting and Dashboards — creating custom reports and dashboards to track testing progress and metrics in Assembla
Integration with Other Tools — integrating Assembla with other tools like JIRA, Trello, and GitHub for seamless collaboration and automation
User Roles and Permissions — managing user roles and permissions to ensure secure access to Assembla features and data

knowledge.what_it_is: TestLink is an open-source test management tool used by QA teams to plan, execute, and report on software testing activities.
what_you_will_learn: Test Plan — creating a structured document outlining test cases, test data, and execution procedures
Test Case — defining a specific scenario or condition to be tested, including preconditions, steps, and expected results
Test Suite — grouping related test cases together for efficient execution and reporting
Test Run — executing a set of test cases within a test suite, with options for manual or automated execution
Defect Tracking — integrating TestLink with defect tracking tools like JIRA or Bugzilla to manage defects found during testing
Reporting and Analytics — generating reports on test coverage, pass/fail rates, and other key metrics to inform testing decisions
User Management — controlling access to TestLink for team members, including roles, permissions, and authentication methods

knowledge.what_it_is: Zephyr is an automated testing tool used by QA teams to manage and execute test cases, created by Pradean (now owned by Perforce). It supports multiple testing frameworks like JUnit, TestNG, and Cucumber, making it a popular choice for Agile and DevOps teams.
what_you_will_learn: Test Case Management — creating, editing, and assigning test cases to test cycles
Test Cycle Management — planning, executing, and reporting on test cycles with automated test runs
Defect Tracking — integrating Zephyr with defect tracking tools like JIRA for seamless issue management
Test Data Management — importing and managing test data from external sources or generating it dynamically
Reporting and Analytics — creating custom reports and dashboards to track test coverage, execution time, and defect density
API Integration — using Zephyr's REST API to integrate with other tools like CI/CD pipelines, version control systems, and project management tools

knowledge.what_it_is: YouTrack is an issue tracking and project management tool developed by JetBrains, widely used in software development teams to manage bugs, features, and tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Creating and managing issues — understanding how to create new issues, assign them to users, and track their status
Issue filtering and searching — learning how to filter and search for specific issues using various criteria such as labels, assignees, and due dates
Customizing workflows — understanding how to create custom workflows and assign tasks to team members based on their roles and responsibilities
Integrating with other tools — learning how to integrate YouTrack with other development tools like Jira, GitHub, and Bitbucket using webhooks and APIs
Using dashboards and reports — understanding how to create custom dashboards and reports to visualize project data and track team performance
Configuring notifications and alerts — learning how to set up notifications and alerts for specific events such as new issues or changes in issue status
Managing users and permissions — understanding how to manage user roles, assign permissions, and control access to features and projects

knowledge.what_it_is: Trello is a visual project management tool that uses Kanban boards to organize tasks and workflows, popular among QA teams due to its flexibility and ease of use.
what_you_will_learn: Board creation — setting up custom boards for different projects or teams
List creation — organizing tasks into lists (To-Do, In Progress, Done) and customizing list settings
Card creation — creating individual cards to represent tasks, including attachments and comments
Task assignment — assigning tasks to team members using @mentions and labels
Due dates and reminders — setting due dates and reminders for tasks and boards
Checklists and subtasks — breaking down large tasks into smaller checklists and subtasks
Integrations with other tools — connecting Trello with other QA tools like Jira, Slack, and GitHub
Custom fields and automation — using Butler automations to streamline workflows and automate repetitive tasks

knowledge.what_it_is: The Agile model is an iterative and incremental software development approach that emphasizes flexibility, collaboration, and continuous improvement. It was first introduced by the Agile Manifesto in 2001 and has since become a widely adopted methodology for software development.
what_you_will_learn: Iterative Development — breaking down work into smaller chunks, delivering working software at regular intervals
Sprint Planning — defining goals and scope for each sprint, ensuring alignment with overall project objectives
Daily Stand-ups — regular team meetings to discuss progress, identify blockers, and align on priorities
Retrospectives — post-sprint reviews to reflect on process improvements, identify areas for growth, and implement changes
Prioritization — using techniques like MoSCoW or Kano to prioritize features and requirements based on business value and risk
Test-Driven Development (TDD) — writing automated tests before implementing code, ensuring test coverage and quality
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) — automating build, test, and deployment processes to ensure rapid feedback and delivery

knowledge.what_it_is: Kanban is an iterative delivery model that focuses on visualizing work, limiting work-in-progress (WIP), and continuous improvement to maximize efficiency and flow. It originated from lean manufacturing principles and has been widely adopted in software development for its flexibility and adaptability.
what_you_will_learn: Board setup — creating a Kanban board with columns representing different stages of work, such as To-Do, In Progress, and Done
WIP limits — setting limits on the number of items that can be in progress at any given time to prevent overloading and improve focus
Prioritization — using techniques like MoSCoW or Kano to prioritize work based on business value and customer needs
Swimlanes — organizing boards by feature, epic, or component to visualize complex workflows and dependencies
Lead time — measuring the time it takes for an item to move from start to finish, including all stages of development and testing
Cycle time — measuring the time spent on each stage of development, excluding waiting times and handoffs
Flow metrics — tracking key performance indicators (KPIs) like throughput, lead time, and cycle time to identify bottlenecks and areas for improvement
Continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) pipeline — integrating Kanban with automated testing, deployment, and monitoring tools to streamline the development process

knowledge.what_it_is: Exploratory testing is an adaptive, investigative approach to software testing that involves simultaneous learning and test design. It's a critical component of the QA role, as it allows testers to identify unknown issues, validate assumptions, and provide valuable feedback to development teams.
what_you_will_learn: Test charters — defining the scope, goals, and risks for exploratory testing sessions
Session-based testing — structuring exploratory testing into focused, time-boxed sessions with clear objectives
Heuristics — using mental models or rules of thumb to guide exploration and identify potential issues
Exploratory testing techniques — applying methods like Equivalence Partitioning, Boundary Value Analysis, and State Transition Testing in an exploratory context
Session notes and summaries — documenting findings, observations, and insights from each session
Risk-based prioritization — identifying high-risk areas to focus exploration and maximize impact
Collaboration with developers — working closely with development teams to understand code changes, identify potential issues, and provide feedback

knowledge.what_it_is: Scrum is an iterative and incremental Agile framework used for managing and completing complex projects, especially software development. It emphasizes teamwork, accountability, and iterative progress toward well-defined goals.
what_you_will_learn: Product Backlog — a prioritized list of features or requirements that the team will work on during each sprint
Sprint Planning — a meeting where the team commits to completing a set of Product Backlog items within a fixed time period (sprint)
Daily Scrum — a 15-minute meeting where team members share their progress, plans, and any obstacles they're facing
Sprint Review — a meeting at the end of each sprint where the team reviews what was completed and demonstrates it to stakeholders
Sprint Retrospective — a meeting after each sprint where the team reflects on what went well and what didn't, and identifies improvements for the next sprint
Scrum Master role — responsible for facilitating Scrum processes, removing impediments, and ensuring the team follows Scrum principles
Product Owner role — responsible for prioritizing the Product Backlog, ensuring it's up-to-date, and making decisions about what to include in each sprint

knowledge.what_it_is: Sanity testing is a type of exploratory testing performed by QA engineers to verify that the most critical features and functionalities are working as expected after a new build or deployment. It's an essential step before moving on to more extensive testing, such as regression testing.
what_you_will_learn: Defining sanity test cases — identifying critical features and creating test scenarios to validate functionality
Prioritizing test cases — determining which tests to run first based on risk and business impact
Sanity testing tools — familiarizing yourself with tools like TestRail, PractiTest, or Zephyr for efficient test case management
Automating sanity tests — using frameworks like Selenium or Cypress to create automated tests that can be run in parallel
Collaboration with developers — working closely with development teams to ensure that the most critical issues are addressed before release
Sanity testing metrics — tracking and analyzing key performance indicators (KPIs) such as test coverage, defect density, and cycle time

knowledge.what_it_is: Extreme Programming (XP) is an iterative and incremental software development methodology that emphasizes technical practices, teamwork, and continuous improvement. It was first introduced by Kent Beck in 1999 and is widely used in Agile development.
what_you_will_learn: Planning Game — a collaborative process to plan the project scope, timeline, and resources
Small Releases — delivering working software in short iterations (usually 1-4 weeks) with frequent feedback
Continuous Integration — integrating code changes into a central repository frequently, often through automated builds and tests
Test-Driven Development (TDD) — writing automated tests before implementing the actual code to ensure it meets requirements
Refactoring — regularly reviewing and improving existing code for better design, structure, and performance
Pair Programming — two developers working together on a single task to improve collaboration, knowledge sharing, and code quality
Collective Code Ownership — all team members are responsible for the entire codebase, promoting shared ownership and accountability
subtopics: infrastructure as code (iac) tools (e.g., terraform, ansible)

knowledge.what_it_is: Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) is an enterprise-level framework for implementing agile methodologies, developed by Dean Leffingwell and Scaled Agile Inc. It provides a structured approach to scaling Scrum and Kanban practices across multiple teams and levels of the organization.
what_you_will_learn: Agile Release Trains (ARTs) — understanding how ARTs enable collaboration and synchronization across teams, with a focus on delivering value to customers
Program Increment (PI) Planning — learning how PI planning enables strategic alignment and prioritization at the program level, including creating a Program Backlog and setting business objectives
Value Streams — identifying and mapping Value Streams to understand the flow of value through the organization, enabling continuous improvement and optimization
SAFe Principles — applying SAFe's guiding principles for Lean-Agile development, such as 'Focus on Flow' and 'Built-in Quality'
Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines — understanding how to implement CI/CD pipelines in a SAFe environment to enable rapid delivery of working software
DevOps practices — learning about DevOps practices such as Infrastructure as Code, Containerization, and Monitoring to support the SAFe framework
SAFe Roles and Responsibilities — understanding the roles and responsibilities within a SAFe team, including Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team members

knowledge.what_it_is: Regression testing is a software testing technique that involves re-running functional or non-functional tests after every code change to ensure that new changes have not introduced any regressions. It's an essential part of the QA role, ensuring that existing functionality still works as expected.
what_you_will_learn: Test automation frameworks — using tools like Selenium WebDriver, Appium, TestNG, or Pytest for automating regression tests
Test data management — creating and maintaining test data sets to ensure consistent results across multiple test runs
Test environment setup — configuring and managing test environments, including virtual machines, containers, or CI/CD pipelines
Defect tracking and reporting — using tools like JIRA, Trello, or Bugzilla for tracking and reporting defects found during regression testing
Test case prioritization — identifying and prioritizing test cases based on risk, business value, or other criteria to ensure maximum coverage with limited resources
Regression testing techniques — applying techniques like equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, or state transition testing to identify potential regressions

knowledge.what_it_is: Test-Driven Development (TDD) is an iterative software development process that relies on the repetitive cycle of writing automated tests before writing the actual code. It ensures that the code is testable, maintainable, and meets the required functionality.
what_you_will_learn: Unit testing — writing individual unit tests for small pieces of code to ensure they work as expected
Test doubles — using mocking libraries like Mockito or Pytest-Mock to isolate dependencies and make tests more efficient
Arrange-Act-Assert pattern — structuring test code to clearly separate setup, execution, and verification phases
Mocking frameworks — understanding the role of tools like Moq, NSubstitute, or EasyMock in creating mock objects for testing
Test coverage metrics — calculating and interpreting metrics like statement, branch, or function coverage to ensure thorough testing
Test-driven refactoring — using TDD principles to refactor code and improve its maintainability and performance
Integration testing — writing tests that verify the interactions between multiple components or systems
Property-based testing — using tools like Hypothesis or QuickCheck to generate test cases based on properties of the system

knowledge.what_it_is: Smoke testing is a type of software testing where a minimal set of tests are executed to verify that critical functionalities work as expected, ensuring that the application is stable and functional enough to proceed with further testing. It's an essential step in the SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle) to prevent downstream defects.
what_you_will_learn: Test planning — creating a test plan to identify smoke tests, prioritize them, and allocate resources
Test case development — designing and writing test cases for critical functionalities, including happy path and edge cases
Test data preparation — creating and managing test data to support smoke testing, considering factors like data consistency and integrity
Smoke testing techniques — applying various techniques such as equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, and state transition testing
Defect tracking and reporting — identifying defects during smoke testing and documenting them for further investigation and resolution
Test environment setup — configuring test environments to support smoke testing, including setting up necessary tools and infrastructure
Smoke testing metrics — measuring the effectiveness of smoke testing, using metrics such as pass/fail rates and defect density

knowledge.what_it_is: Unit testing is a software development process that involves writing and executing small, isolated pieces of code (units) to ensure they function as expected, typically using automated tools. In QA, unit testing helps catch defects early on, reducing downstream costs and improving overall quality.
what_you_will_learn: Test-Driven Development (TDD) — writing tests before actual code, driving development through red-green-refactor cycles
Unit test frameworks: JUnit, TestNG, PyUnit, Unittest — understanding the strengths and weaknesses of each for different languages and use cases
Mocking libraries: Mockito, Moq, EasyMock — learning to isolate dependencies and write more robust unit tests
Dependency injection: using frameworks like Spring or Guice to decouple dependencies and make code more testable
Unit testing in Agile and Scrum — integrating unit testing into the development workflow for continuous improvement
Code coverage metrics: JaCoCo, Cobertura, Clover — understanding how to measure and improve code coverage for better testing
Test smells: detecting and refactoring unit tests that are too complex or tightly coupled with production code

knowledge.what_it_is: The V-Model is a software development process model that emphasizes testing and quality assurance throughout the entire development lifecycle, with a focus on ensuring that each phase of the development process is thoroughly tested before moving on to the next one. It is commonly used in the QA role for its emphasis on testing and validation.
what_you_will_learn: Requirements gathering — identifying and documenting user requirements through techniques like use cases and user stories
Design review — reviewing system design documents to ensure they meet functional and non-functional requirements
Unit testing — writing automated tests for individual units of code, such as functions or methods
Integration testing — testing how multiple units of code integrate together to form a cohesive whole
System testing — testing the entire system to ensure it meets all user requirements and works as expected
Acceptance testing — verifying that the system meets the acceptance criteria defined by stakeholders
Test case development — creating detailed test cases based on functional and non-functional requirements
Test data management — managing and maintaining test data to support testing activities

knowledge.what_it_is: The Waterfall model is a linear and sequential software development approach where each phase is completed before moving to the next one, with minimal overlap or iteration.
what_you_will_learn: Requirements gathering — collecting and documenting functional and non-functional requirements from stakeholders
Analysis — breaking down requirements into smaller components, identifying dependencies and data flows
Design — creating detailed design documents outlining system architecture, component interactions and interfaces
Implementation — writing code based on the design document, with a focus on meeting specific requirements
Testing — executing manual or automated tests to ensure the software meets the specified requirements
Deployment — releasing the software to production, including setup of infrastructure, configuration and monitoring
Maintenance — ongoing support and updates to fix bugs, add new features and improve performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Test cases and scenarios are detailed descriptions of how to test software applications, including inputs, expected results, and any preconditions or assumptions.
what_you_will_learn: Test case structure — understanding the components of a well-crafted test case, including test data, steps, and expected results
Test scenario planning — creating high-level scenarios that cover multiple test cases and user journeys
Equivalence partitioning — identifying and testing representative inputs within each equivalence partition
Boundary value analysis — testing values at the boundaries of each input range or field
State transition testing — verifying that the application behaves correctly during state transitions, such as login and logout
Error guessing — using experience and knowledge to identify potential error scenarios and test cases
Test case prioritization — determining which test cases are most critical and should be executed first
Automated test case generation — using tools like Selenium or Appium to generate automated tests from manual test cases

knowledge.what_it_is: Ensuring that software applications function as expected across different environments, browsers, and devices, without compromising performance or security.
what_you_will_learn: Cross-browser testing — ensuring application functionality works consistently across Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge, and Internet Explorer
Device compatibility testing — verifying app behavior on various mobile and desktop operating systems (iOS, Android, Windows, macOS)
Browser versioning — understanding how to test for different browser versions and their impact on application performance
Screen reader accessibility testing — ensuring applications are accessible to users with visual impairments using screen readers like JAWS or VoiceOver
Mobile emulation vs. real device testing — choosing the right approach for mobile compatibility testing (e.g., using tools like Appium, Espresso)
Browser extensions and plugins testing — understanding how browser add-ons can impact application behavior and performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Accessibility testing ensures that digital products are usable by people with disabilities, following Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) and Section 508 standards. It's a critical aspect of QA to ensure inclusive design.
what_you_will_learn: WCAG guidelines — understanding the four principles of accessibility: perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust
Screen reader testing — using tools like NVDA or JAWS to simulate screen reader experience for visually impaired users
Keyboard-only navigation — ensuring that all interactive elements can be accessed using only a keyboard
Color contrast analysis — using tools like Snook or WebAIM to evaluate color contrast between text and background
Image description testing — verifying that alt text is descriptive, concise, and accurate for images with no alternative text
Aria attributes testing — ensuring that ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes are correctly implemented for dynamic content
Focus order testing — verifying that the focus order of interactive elements follows a logical sequence
WCAG 2.1 success criteria — understanding the updated guidelines for accessibility, including mobile-specific requirements

knowledge.what_it_is: Integration testing is a software testing technique that verifies the interactions between different components or modules of an application, ensuring they work together as expected.
what_you_will_learn: API testing — using tools like Postman, cURL, or API testing frameworks (e.g., Pytest-requests) to verify API endpoints and data exchange
Mocking libraries (e.g., Mockito, Moq) — creating mock objects to isolate dependencies and test interactions between components
Service virtualization (e.g., SoapUI, Postman) — simulating external services or APIs for testing purposes
Test-driven development (TDD) with integration testing — writing tests before code and using frameworks like JUnit or Pytest to ensure integration
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines — automating integration testing as part of the build process using tools like Jenkins, Travis CI, or CircleCI
API mocking frameworks (e.g., WireMock, Mockk) — creating mock APIs for testing purposes and verifying API interactions
Service-oriented architecture (SOA) testing — testing microservices and their interactions in a distributed system

knowledge.what_it_is: Verification and validation are critical processes in software testing that ensure the product meets requirements and works as expected. Verification checks if the product is built correctly, while validation ensures it meets user needs.
what_you_will_learn: Black box testing — testing without knowledge of internal workings, focusing on inputs and outputs
White box testing — testing with knowledge of internal workings, focusing on code coverage and paths
Grey box testing — combination of black and white box testing, using partial knowledge of internal workings
Equivalence partitioning — dividing input data into partitions to reduce test cases
Boundary value analysis — testing at boundaries between partitions for edge cases
State transition testing — testing how the product behaves during state changes
Decision table testing — testing all possible combinations of conditions and actions
Use case testing — testing scenarios based on user interactions and requirements

knowledge.what_it_is: Test planning is a critical phase of the software development lifecycle (SDLC) where QA engineers define the scope, objectives, and deliverables of testing activities to ensure that the product meets the required quality standards.
what_you_will_learn: Test Planning Document — a comprehensive document outlining test objectives, scope, schedule, and resources
Test Scope Statement — defining what is included or excluded from testing based on business requirements and risk assessment
Risk-Based Testing (RBT) — identifying high-risk areas of the product and prioritizing testing efforts accordingly
Test Case Prioritization — determining which test cases to execute first, based on risk, complexity, and business value
Test Data Management — planning for data creation, storage, and maintenance throughout the testing process
Test Environment Setup — defining the necessary hardware, software, and network configurations for testing
Test Metrics and KPIs — establishing key performance indicators to measure test effectiveness and efficiency
Defect Classification and Severity — categorizing defects based on their impact on the product's functionality and usability
subtopics: test data management tools (e.g. test data manager, excel)
test environment setup scripts (e.g. ansible, terraform)
test metrics and kpis (e.g. test coverage, defect density)
defect classification and severity levels (e.g. critical, major, minor)

knowledge.what_it_is: User Acceptance Testing (UAT) is a critical phase of software development where end-users validate that the application meets their requirements and expectations. It's a collaborative effort between QA, developers, and stakeholders to ensure the product is usable, efficient, and meets business needs.
what_you_will_learn: UAT test case creation — writing detailed test cases based on user stories and acceptance criteria
Test data management — creating and managing test data for UAT, including data masking and sanitization
User role mapping — defining user roles and permissions to ensure accurate testing
Acceptance criteria evaluation — verifying that the application meets all acceptance criteria defined by stakeholders
Defect tracking and reporting — identifying, documenting, and escalating defects found during UAT
Test environment setup and configuration — ensuring the UAT environment is properly set up and configured for testing
User involvement and feedback — engaging end-users in the testing process to gather feedback and validate assumptions

knowledge.what_it_is: Non-functional testing (NFT) focuses on evaluating system performance, usability, and reliability without focusing on functional requirements. It's crucial for ensuring that software systems meet user expectations and can handle various workloads.
what_you_will_learn: Performance metrics — understanding key indicators like response time, throughput, and resource utilization
Load testing tools — familiarizing with Apache JMeter, Gatling, Locust, and LoadRunner for simulating large workloads
Stress testing techniques — learning to identify system bottlenecks using stress testing methods like incremental load and spike testing
Scalability testing strategies — understanding how to evaluate a system's ability to handle increased traffic or user growth
Usability testing methodologies — applying human-centered design principles to ensure intuitive interfaces and minimal user frustration
Reliability testing techniques — identifying potential failure points using fault injection, error handling, and recovery mechanisms
Security testing frameworks — understanding OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, and other tools for detecting vulnerabilities and ensuring secure coding practices

knowledge.what_it_is: Load testing is a type of performance testing that simulates a large number of users or requests to an application, measuring its response time, throughput, and resource utilization. It helps identify bottlenecks, optimize system resources, and ensure scalability.
what_you_will_learn: Apache JMeter — a popular open-source load testing tool for web applications, supporting HTTP/HTTPS protocols
Gatling — a commercial load testing tool with high-level DSL (Domain Specific Language) for easy scripting and reporting
Locust — a Python-based load testing tool for simulating user behavior and measuring performance under heavy loads
LoadUI — a cloud-based load testing platform for web applications, supporting multiple protocols and integrations
RPM (Requests Per Minute) — a key metric for measuring application throughput and response time under load
Resource utilization monitoring — understanding CPU, memory, disk usage, and network I/O during load testing
Identifying bottlenecks — using tools like New Relic, Datadog, or Prometheus to analyze performance data and optimize resources

knowledge.what_it_is: Performance testing is a type of software testing that evaluates how well an application performs under various loads, such as high traffic, concurrent users, and resource constraints. It helps ensure that the system can handle expected usage patterns without degrading performance or crashing.
what_you_will_learn: Load testing — simulating multiple users to measure response times, throughput, and resource utilization (e.g., Apache JMeter, Gatling)
Stress testing — pushing an application beyond its normal capacity to identify breaking points (e.g., Apache JMeter, Locust)
Endurance testing — running load tests for extended periods to evaluate system stability under sustained loads
Spike testing — simulating sudden spikes in traffic or usage patterns to measure performance degradation
Scalability testing — evaluating how well an application scales with increased resources (e.g., adding more servers, databases)
Resource utilization monitoring — tracking CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network resource consumption using tools like Prometheus, Grafana
Performance metrics analysis — interpreting key performance indicators (KPIs) such as response time, throughput, error rates, and resource utilization
Capacity planning — determining the required resources to handle expected loads and ensure system scalability

knowledge.what_it_is: Stress testing is a type of non-functional testing that evaluates how software performs under extreme loads, such as high traffic or concurrent user activity, to identify performance bottlenecks and ensure scalability. It's essential for QA engineers to simulate heavy usage scenarios to guarantee the system can handle production workloads.
what_you_will_learn: Load testing tools — using Apache JMeter, Gatling, Locust, or NeoLoad to simulate user traffic and measure response times
Stress testing techniques — understanding how to create realistic load profiles, including ramp-up/ramp-down, steady-state, and spike testing
Performance metrics — identifying key indicators of system performance, such as throughput, latency, CPU usage, memory consumption, and error rates
Scalability testing — evaluating the ability of the system to handle increased loads by adding more resources or instances
Capacity planning — determining the maximum number of users or transactions a system can handle within a given timeframe
Flood testing — simulating an extreme load to identify system limits and potential bottlenecks
Soak testing — running continuous load tests for extended periods to evaluate system stability under sustained loads

knowledge.what_it_is: Security testing is the process of identifying vulnerabilities and weaknesses in software applications, systems, or networks to prevent unauthorized access, data breaches, or other security threats. As a QA engineer, it's essential to perform security testing to ensure the application meets industry standards and regulatory requirements.
what_you_will_learn: OWASP Top 10 — understanding the most critical web application security risks, including injection attacks, cross-site scripting (XSS), and sensitive data exposure
Vulnerability scanning — using tools like Nessus, Qualys, or Burp Suite to identify potential vulnerabilities in the application's code, configuration, or dependencies
Penetration testing — simulating real-world attacks on the application to test its defenses and identify weaknesses
Static Application Security Testing (SAST) — analyzing source code for security issues using tools like SonarQube, Veracode, or Checkmarx
Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) — scanning running applications for security vulnerabilities using tools like ZAP, Burp Suite, or Acunetix
Security testing frameworks — understanding the use of frameworks like OWASP ZAP, Selenium WebDriver, or Appium to automate security testing
Fuzz testing — injecting invalid or unexpected input data to test an application's robustness and error handling
Compliance testing — ensuring the application meets regulatory requirements, such as PCI-DSS for payment card industry compliance

knowledge.what_it_is: Frontend automation refers to the process of automating interactions with web applications using various tools and frameworks, enabling efficient testing, validation, and maintenance of frontend code. This involves simulating user interactions, verifying expected outcomes, and reporting defects or issues.
what_you_will_learn: Cypress — a popular JavaScript-based end-to-end testing framework for automating complex interactions with web applications
Playwright — an open-source browser automation library that supports multiple browsers and provides a high-level API for automating frontend functionality
Webdriver.io — a Node.js-based test automation framework that uses the WebDriver protocol to interact with web browsers and automate frontend tasks
Selenium WebDriver — a widely-used tool for automating web browsers, supporting various programming languages and frameworks for frontend testing
Page Object Model (POM) — a design pattern for organizing and structuring frontend automation code using page objects and reusable methods
Test Data Management (TDM) — strategies and techniques for managing test data, including data generation, parameterization, and data-driven testing
Assertions and Verifications — writing effective assertions and verifications to validate expected outcomes in frontend automation tests
Frontend Automation Frameworks — design patterns and best practices for structuring and organizing frontend automation code using frameworks like Cypress or Playwright

knowledge.what_it_is: Cypress is an open-source, JavaScript-based end-to-end testing framework used by QA engineers to automate web applications. It provides a simple and intuitive API for writing tests that interact with web pages, simulating real user interactions.
what_you_will_learn: Command types — declaring commands using the `cy` command, including assertions, actions, and custom commands
Chaining commands — combining multiple Cypress commands to perform complex actions on a single line of code
Aliases — creating shortcuts for frequently used values or expressions in tests
Variables — declaring and accessing variables within test files, including session storage and local storage
Timeouts — configuring timeouts for individual commands or the entire test run
Network requests — simulating network requests using Cypress's `cy.request()` command
Assertions — verifying expected outcomes using assertions like `expect()`, `should()`, and `assert()`
Fixtures — loading pre-defined data into tests using Cypress's `cy.fixture()` command

knowledge.what_it_is: Playwright is an open-source, multi-language browser automation framework developed by Microsoft, designed to automate web browsers such as Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit. It provides a high-level API for automating complex user interactions, including scrolling, clicking, and filling out forms.
what_you_will_learn: Browser instances — creating and managing multiple browser sessions in parallel
Contexts — switching between different contexts within the same browser instance (e.g., incognito mode)
Pages — interacting with web pages using a high-level API for elements, properties, and methods
Selectors — using CSS selectors to identify elements on the page and perform actions
Actions — performing complex user interactions such as scrolling, clicking, and hovering
Expectations — asserting expected conditions on the page using a fluent API
Page objects — encapsulating page-specific logic and behavior for easier maintenance
Type and click methods — simulating keyboard input and mouse clicks on elements

knowledge.what_it_is: Webdriver.io is an open-source, Node.js-based test automation framework that supports multiple programming languages and browsers. It's widely used by QA teams to create end-to-end browser automation tests.
what_you_will_learn: WebDriverIO API — interacting with the WebDriver protocol using a JavaScript API
BrowserStack — running cross-browser tests on real devices in the cloud
Selenium WebDriver — integrating Webdriver.io with Selenium for browser automation
Page Object Model (POM) — organizing test code into reusable page objects
Test Runner — executing test suites using the wdio command-line interface or programmatically
Config Files — managing test configuration using JSON files and environment variables
Reporter Plugins — customizing test report output with plugins like Allure, JUnit, or HTML Reporter

knowledge.what_it_is: Selenium is an open-source tool for automating web browsers, used by QA engineers to test web applications across multiple platforms and browsers.
what_you_will_learn: WebDriver API — interacting with web elements using Selenium's programming language bindings (Java, Python, C#, Ruby)
Browser interactions — navigating to URLs, clicking buttons, filling forms, submitting forms
Element locators — identifying web elements using various strategies (ID, XPath, CSS selector, link text)
Wait statements — handling dynamic content and asynchronous operations (WebDriverWait, ExpectedConditions)
Alerts and pop-ups — handling browser alerts, confirmations, and prompts
Cookies management — reading and writing cookies for authentication or data storage purposes
Selenium Grid — distributing tests across multiple machines and browsers for parallel execution

knowledge.what_it_is: Jasmine is a popular open-source testing framework for JavaScript, used by QA engineers to write and run unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests. It supports both synchronous and asynchronous testing and provides features like mocking, stubbing, and spying.
what_you_will_learn: Test suites — organizing related tests into a single file or directory
Specs — individual test cases written using Jasmine's BDD syntax (describe, it)
Expectations — assertions used to verify expected behavior (toBe, toEqual, toThrow)
Matchers — custom assertion functions for specific types of values (toContain, toBeTruthy)
Spies and stubs — mocking dependencies to isolate unit under test
Async testing — writing tests that interact with asynchronous code using Jasmine's done() function
Jasmine spies — creating and using spies to track method calls and return values

knowledge.what_it_is: Jest is an open-source, widely-used testing framework developed by Facebook (Meta), designed specifically for JavaScript applications. It provides a simple and intuitive API for writing unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests.
what_you_will_learn: Test suites — organizing related test files into logical groups using Jest's built-in `describe` function
Expectations — asserting expected behavior of code using Jest's `expect` function (e.g., `expect(add(2, 2)).toBe(4)`)
Mocking — replacing dependencies with mock implementations to isolate and test specific components or functions
Spies — intercepting and verifying function calls, including arguments and return values
Setup/teardown — running setup code before each test and teardown code after each test using Jest's `beforeEach` and `afterEach` functions
Async testing — writing tests for asynchronous code using Jest's built-in `test` function with the `async` keyword
Snapshot testing — automatically generating and verifying snapshots of component output using Jest's `snapshotTest` function

knowledge.what_it_is: Nightwatch.js is an open-source, headless browser testing framework written in JavaScript, designed to automate web application testing with ease and flexibility.
what_you_will_learn: Setting up Nightwatch — installing dependencies, configuring test environment, and writing first test
Using Nightwatch API — understanding the basic commands (get, element, elements), selectors, and assertions
Writing Nightwatch tests — using Nightwatch's built-in functions (url, title, element), and custom commands
Handling web elements — using Nightwatch's APIs for interacting with DOM elements (click, sendKeys, clearValue)
Working with Nightwatch's client API — understanding the client object, its methods, and how to use it in tests
Using Nightwatch's built-in plugins — such as selenium-webdriver, chromedriver, geckodriver, and edge-driver
Advanced topics: using Nightwatch with CI/CD pipelines, integrating with other testing frameworks (e.g., Cypress), and optimizing test execution

knowledge.what_it_is: Puppeteer is a Node.js library developed by the Chrome team that provides a high-level API to control headless Chrome or Chromium browsers, enabling automated testing and automation of web applications.
what_you_will_learn: Page object model — creating reusable page objects with Puppeteer for efficient test maintenance
Browser launch options — configuring browser settings like headless mode, args, and executablePath
Wait strategies — using waitForSelector, waitForFunction, and waitForResponse to handle asynchronous interactions
Element handling — using methods like click, type, and selectOption to interact with web elements
Page navigation — navigating between pages using methods like goto, back, and forward
Cookie management — setting, getting, and deleting cookies using the cookie API
Network requests — inspecting and manipulating network requests using the puppeteer.network API

knowledge.what_it_is: In QA, HTML, CSS, and JavaScript are used to create web applications that can be automated using various testing frameworks and tools.
what_you_will_learn: DOM manipulation — accessing and modifying HTML elements using JavaScript
CSS selectors — understanding how to target specific HTML elements with CSS rules (e.g., `#id`, `.class`, `[attribute]`)
JavaScript events — handling user interactions like clicks, mouseovers, and key presses
AJAX requests — making asynchronous HTTP requests to fetch data from servers using JavaScript
CSS preprocessors — understanding how tools like Sass or Less can enhance CSS development
JavaScript frameworks — familiarizing yourself with popular libraries like jQuery or Lodash for simplifying DOM interactions
Browser automation APIs — learning about APIs like `document` and `window` that allow you to automate browser interactions

knowledge.what_it_is: Static Web Applications (SWAs), Progressive Web Apps (PWAs), and JAMStack are modern web development approaches that prioritize speed, security, and scalability. SWAs use serverless architectures to deliver static content, PWAs leverage browser capabilities for offline access and native app-like experiences, while JAMStack combines static site generation with APIs and CDNs for efficient delivery.
what_you_will_learn: Static Site Generation (SSG) — using tools like Next.js, Gatsby, or Hugo to pre-build and deploy websites
Serverless Architecture — leveraging AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, or Azure Functions for stateless computations
Service Workers — implementing offline support, caching, and push notifications in PWAs with modern browsers
PWA Manifest — defining metadata for web apps, including icons, name, description, and launch screens
JAMStack Architecture — combining static site generation, APIs (e.g., GraphQL), and CDNs (e.g., Cloudflare) for efficient delivery
API-first Development — designing and building APIs as the primary interface for JAMStack applications
CDN Caching Strategies — optimizing content delivery networks for faster page loads and reduced latency
Security Best Practices — implementing HTTPS, Content Security Policy (CSP), and Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) in SWAs and PWAs

knowledge.what_it_is: Client-Side Rendering (CSR) and Server-Side Rendering (SSR) are rendering strategies used in web development to improve page loading speed, SEO, and user experience. CSR is a traditional approach where the client's browser renders the UI, while SSR is a modern approach where the server generates the initial HTML.
what_you_will_learn: CSR vs SSR: understanding the trade-offs between client-side rendering and server-side rendering
Server-Side Rendering (SSR) with Next.js — using getServerSideProps to pre-render pages on the server
Static Site Generation (SSG) with Gatsby — using createPages to generate static HTML for SEO-friendly sites
Client-Side Rendering (CSR) with React — understanding how React uses reconciliation and virtual DOM diffing to optimize rendering
Hybrid Rendering — combining CSR and SSR to improve page loading speed and user experience
Rendering Strategies in Webdriver.io — using waitFor and expect to wait for elements to be rendered on the client-side
Server-Side Rendering with Cypress — using cy.visit() to test server-rendered pages
Client-Side Rendering with Puppeteer — using page.goto() to render pages on the client-side

knowledge.what_it_is: In web development, Responsive and Adaptive are design approaches to ensure websites or applications work seamlessly across various devices, screen sizes, and orientations. As a QA, understanding these concepts is crucial to identify and report issues related to layout, usability, and accessibility.
what_you_will_learn: Responsive Design — using flexible grids, images, and media queries to adapt to different screen sizes and orientations
Media Queries — writing CSS rules that apply based on specific conditions such as screen size, device type, or orientation
Mobile-First Approach — designing for smaller screens first and then scaling up for larger ones
Breakpoints — defining specific screen widths where the layout changes to accommodate different devices
Fluid vs Fixed Layouts — understanding when to use fluid (percentage-based) or fixed layouts for better responsiveness
CSS Grid and Flexbox — using modern CSS techniques to create flexible, responsive layouts
Responsiveness Testing Tools — familiarizing yourself with tools like Responsinator, BrowserStack, or online emulators

knowledge.what_it_is: AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML) is a technique used to update parts of an HTML document without requiring a full page reload, typically used by web applications to improve user experience. It involves making HTTP requests from JavaScript code using the XMLHttpRequest object or the Fetch API.
what_you_will_learn: XMLHttpRequest — the original JavaScript object for sending HTTP requests, now deprecated in favor of newer APIs
Fetch API — a modern replacement for XMLHttpRequest, providing a simpler and more powerful way to make HTTP requests
Promise-based programming — using promises to handle asynchronous operations and avoid callback hell
async/await syntax — writing synchronous-looking code that's actually asynchronous under the hood, making it easier to read and maintain
JSON data formats — working with JSON data structures, including parsing, serialization, and deserialization
Error handling in AJAX requests — understanding how to catch and handle errors, such as network failures or server-side errors
AJAX request headers — setting custom headers for AJAX requests, including authentication tokens and content negotiation

knowledge.what_it_is: Caching is a technique used by QA engineers to improve application performance and reduce load times by storing frequently accessed data or results of expensive operations in a temporary storage location, such as memory or disk.
what_you_will_learn: Cache invalidation strategies — techniques for ensuring cache consistency when data changes, including time-to-live (TTL), versioning, and event-driven invalidation
Cache types — understanding the differences between in-memory caching (e.g., Redis, Memcached), disk-based caching (e.g., Hazelcast, Ehcache), and hybrid caching solutions
Cache key management — strategies for generating and managing cache keys to ensure efficient lookup and storage of cached data
Cache expiration policies — implementing policies for removing stale or outdated cache entries, such as least recently used (LRU) or most frequently accessed (MFA)
Cache sizing and optimization — techniques for determining optimal cache sizes and optimizing cache performance in terms of hit ratio, miss rate, and latency
Caching frameworks and libraries — using popular caching frameworks like Spring Cache, Guava Cache, or Apache Ignite to simplify caching configuration and management
Monitoring and debugging caching issues — tools and techniques for identifying and resolving caching-related problems, such as cache misses, stale data, or performance bottlenecks

knowledge.what_it_is: Selenium IDE is an open-source, record-and-playback tool for automating web browsers, developed by Mozilla. It allows users to create and edit test scripts using a user-friendly interface.
what_you_will_learn: Recording and playing back browser interactions — capturing user actions on the web page
Editing recorded scripts in Selenium IDE's editor — adding pauses, loops, and conditional statements
Using Selenium WebDriver API — understanding how to interact with elements using Java/Python/C#
Identifying and interacting with web elements — using XPath, CSS selectors, and ID
Handling alerts and pop-ups — dismissing or accepting them in test scripts
Working with frames and iframes — navigating and interacting within nested frames
Using Selenium's built-in commands — such as navigate, back, forward, refresh, and close

knowledge.what_it_is: Ghost Inspector is an AI-powered automated testing and debugging tool for web applications, providing end-to-end visual testing and issue detection capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Recording and playback — creating test cases by recording user interactions and playing them back to identify issues
Visual testing — using AI-powered image recognition to detect visual changes in the application
Automated debugging — identifying and fixing errors with Ghost Inspector's automated debugging features
Test case management — organizing and managing test cases within the Ghost Inspector platform
Integration with CI/CD pipelines — automating tests as part of the continuous integration and delivery process
API testing — using Ghost Inspector to test APIs and microservices
Cross-browser testing — ensuring application functionality across multiple browsers and versions
Test data management — managing test data within the Ghost Inspector platform

knowledge.what_it_is: A Bug Magnet is a technique used by QA engineers to intentionally introduce bugs into the application under test, making it easier to identify and reproduce issues.
what_you_will_learn: Identifying high-risk areas of the codebase — focusing on critical components and APIs
Creating intentional defects in specific scenarios, such as edge cases or error handling
Using tools like Selenium WebDriver, Appium, or TestComplete to inject bugs into the application
Writing test cases that simulate real-world user interactions, including keyboard and mouse events
Understanding how to use mocking libraries like Mockito or Pytest-Mock to isolate dependencies
Analyzing stack traces and error messages to identify root causes of issues
Developing a bug magnet strategy for different types of applications, such as web, mobile, or desktop

knowledge.what_it_is: Karate Framework is an open-source, BDD (Behavior-Driven Development) framework for API testing, built on top of Cucumber and Selenium. It's designed to simplify API testing by allowing developers to write tests in a natural language style, similar to Gherkin.
what_you_will_learn: Feature files — writing test scenarios in a human-readable format using Gherkin syntax
Step definitions — mapping feature file steps to Java code using @Given, @When, and @Then annotations
Karate API client — interacting with APIs using the Karate API client, which provides methods for sending requests and assertions
Request and response validation — using matchers like 'contains', 'matches', and 'not' to validate request and response data
Parameterization — using karate.config or feature file parameters to reuse test data across multiple scenarios
Data-driven testing — using CSV, JSON, or XML files to drive test data from external sources
Parallel execution — running tests in parallel using Karate's built-in support for parallel execution
Reporting and logging — generating reports and logs using Karate's built-in reporting features

knowledge.what_it_is: Browser/Dev Tools are essential software applications used by Quality Assurance (QA) engineers to inspect, debug, and optimize web applications. They provide a range of features, including element inspection, network request monitoring, performance profiling, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Chrome DevTools — the industry-standard browser tool for debugging and optimizing web applications, featuring Elements panel, Sources panel, Network panel, Performance panel, and more
Inspecting DOM elements using Chrome DevTools' Elements panel, including selecting elements, viewing element properties, and editing attributes
Using the Console panel in Chrome DevTools to log messages, inspect variables, and execute JavaScript code
Monitoring network requests with the Network panel in Chrome DevTools, including analyzing request headers, response bodies, and performance metrics
Profiling web application performance using the Performance panel in Chrome DevTools, including identifying bottlenecks, optimizing resource usage, and improving page load times
Using the Sources panel in Chrome DevTools to set breakpoints, step through code, and inspect variables during runtime
Debugging JavaScript errors with the Debugger tab in Chrome DevTools, including setting breakpoints, stepping through code, and viewing call stacks

knowledge.what_it_is: Soap UI is an open-source API testing tool used by QA engineers to test web services, APIs, and microservices. It provides a user-friendly interface for creating and executing tests, and supports multiple protocols such as HTTP/1.1, HTTPS, JMS, AMF, and more.
what_you_will_learn: API Testing — testing web services, APIs, and microservices using Soap UI
Test Creation — creating test cases in Soap UI, including request and response assertions
Test Execution — executing tests in Soap UI, including support for multiple protocols and authentication methods
Mocking — mocking dependencies in Soap UI to isolate and test specific components or services
API Mocking — using Soap UI's API mocking feature to simulate responses from external APIs
Assertion Statements — writing assertion statements in Soap UI to verify expected outcomes

load testing with soap ui pro
performance testing with soap ui pro
api security testing with soap ui pro

knowledge.what_it_is: Postman and Newman are popular tools used by QA engineers to automate API testing, mocking, and documentation. Postman is a user-friendly interface for building and sending HTTP requests, while Newman is a command-line tool that allows you to run your Postman collections in CI/CD pipelines.
what_you_will_learn: Creating and organizing APIs using Postman's workspace feature — a centralized hub for all your API-related work
Writing and running API tests using the Newman command-line tool, including test suites, assertions, and error handling
Using Postman's built-in features such as pre-request scripts, tests, and environment variables to parameterize and customize your API requests

Generating API documentation using Postman's built-in documentation feature, including auto-generated API reference pages and code snippets
Using Newman's `--reporter` option to generate detailed test reports in various formats such as JSON, HTML, or CSV
Integrating Postman with CI/CD tools like Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, or CircleCI using Newman's command-line interface

knowledge.what_it_is: QA Wolf is an open-source, Python-based test automation framework designed specifically for QA engineers to create and execute automated tests with ease.
what_you_will_learn: Test discovery — using keywords and regular expressions to identify test cases in the codebase
Test creation — writing test scripts using Python and the Wolf API to interact with the application under test
Test execution — running test suites and individual tests, with support for parallel execution and reporting
Assertion library — using Wolf's built-in assertion library to verify expected results and handle failures
Reporting and logging — generating reports and logs in various formats, including HTML, CSV, and JSON
Integration with CI/CD tools — automating test runs and reporting within Jenkins, Travis CI, or other CI/CD pipelines
Custom plugins — extending Wolf's functionality using Python plugins to integrate with custom tools and frameworks

knowledge.what_it_is: A robot is an autonomous machine that can perform tasks with precision and speed, commonly used in Quality Assurance (QA) to automate repetitive testing processes.
what_you_will_learn: Test Automation Frameworks — using tools like Selenium, Appium, or Robot Framework to create test scripts
Keyword-Driven Testing — creating reusable keywords for common actions and reducing test maintenance efforts
Data-Driven Testing — using external data sources to feed test inputs and outputs, improving test flexibility and maintainability
Test Scripting Languages — writing tests in languages like Python, Java, or C#, including syntax and best practices
Robot Framework Libraries — utilizing libraries like SeleniumLibrary, AppiumLibrary, or RESTLibrary for efficient testing
Test Data Management — understanding how to manage and update test data, including data formats and storage options

knowledge.what_it_is: REST Assured is an open-source Java library used for API testing, providing a simple and intuitive way to create test cases for RESTful web services. It supports various protocols like HTTP/1.1, HTTPS, and WebSocket.
what_you_will_learn: API Requests — sending HTTP requests using methods like GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc., with parameters and headers
Response Assertions — verifying response status codes, headers, content types, and body contents using various matchers

knowledge.what_it_is: Accessibility tests are automated checks to ensure digital products (websites, web applications) meet accessibility standards and guidelines, such as WCAG 2.1, Section 508, and ADA. These tests help identify and fix issues that make it difficult for people with disabilities to use the product.

Automated testing tools — using tools like Lighthouse, WAVE, Axe, and Pa11y to identify accessibility issues
Manual testing techniques — learning how to perform manual checks for accessibility issues, such as keyboard-only navigation and screen reader testing

knowledge.what_it_is: Security testing is an essential aspect of Quality Assurance (QA) that involves identifying vulnerabilities and weaknesses in software applications, systems, or networks to ensure they meet security standards and regulations.

Penetration testing — simulating real-world attacks on applications and systems to test defenses and identify weaknesses

Dynamic application security testing (DAST) — scanning running applications for security vulnerabilities using tools like AppScan, Acunetix, or ZAP

Compliance testing — ensuring software and systems meet regulatory requirements such as PCI-DSS, HIPAA, or GDPR
