Git tags — creating and managing version labels for specific points in project history (e.g., release versions)
Git hooks — customizing repository behavior with scripts that run at specific events (e.g., commit, push)
Git blame — identifying who last modified a particular line of code or file
Git bisect — efficiently finding the commit that introduced a bug by performing a binary search

knowledge.what_it_is: GitHub is a web-based platform for version control and collaboration on software development projects, widely used by QA teams to manage test cases, track bugs, and collaborate with developers.
what_you_will_learn: Git basics — understanding commit messages, branches, merges, and pull requests
Creating and managing repositories — setting up a new repository, adding files, committing changes, and pushing to GitHub
Branching strategies — implementing feature branches, release branches, and hotfix branches for efficient testing and deployment
Pull request management — creating, reviewing, and merging pull requests to ensure code quality and collaboration
Code review best practices — using GitHub's built-in code review tools to provide feedback on code changes
Integrating with CI/CD pipelines — automating testing and deployment processes using GitHub Actions and other integrations
Using GitHub Issues for bug tracking — creating, assigning, and prioritizing issues to manage the testing process

knowledge.what_it_is: Bitbucket is a web-based source code repository manager hosted by Atlassian, used for version control and collaboration among developers and QA teams.
what_you_will_learn: Repository management — creating, cloning, and managing repositories for testing and development
Branching strategies — understanding the concept of branches, feature branching, release branching, and hotfix branching
Merging and resolving conflicts — using Bitbucket's built-in merge tools to resolve conflicts and manage changes
Code reviews — participating in code review processes using Bitbucket's pull request features
Test automation integration — integrating test automation frameworks like Selenium, Appium, or TestNG with Bitbucket
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines — setting up CI/CD pipelines using tools like Jenkins, Travis CI, or CircleCI
Security and access control — managing user permissions, roles, and access levels for secure collaboration
Bitbucket API integration — using the Bitbucket REST API to automate tasks, fetch data, and integrate with other tools

knowledge.what_it_is: GitLab is an open-source DevOps platform that provides source code management, continuous integration and delivery, and project management tools for software development teams.
what_you_will_learn: Creating and managing projects — setting up a new project, adding members, and configuring permissions
Branching strategies — understanding the concept of feature branches, release branches, and hotfix branches
Merging code — using GitLab's merge request feature to review and approve changes before merging into the main branch
Code reviews — participating in code reviews, providing feedback, and approving or rejecting changes
CI/CD pipelines — creating and managing pipelines for automated testing, building, and deployment of software
Environment variables — using environment variables to store sensitive data and make it available to CI/CD pipelines
GitLab CI/CD templates — using pre-built templates to create and manage CI/CD pipelines
Security scanning — running security scans on code repositories to identify vulnerabilities and weaknesses

knowledge.what_it_is: Drones are unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) used for various purposes such as inspection, surveillance, and data collection. In QA, drones are used to test and validate software applications that interact with drone-related systems.
what_you_will_learn: Drone APIs — understanding the programming interfaces for interacting with drone hardware and software, including DJI SDK, PX4, and MAVLink
Flight Control Systems — learning about the autopilot systems used in drones, including ArduPilot and PX4
Sensor Integration — integrating sensors such as GPS, accelerometers, and gyroscopes to collect data from drones
Drone Simulation Tools — using tools like DroneKit-SITL, QGroundControl, and PX4 SITL to simulate drone behavior and test software
Autopilot Systems Testing — testing the autopilot systems used in drones, including ArduPilot and PX4
Sensor Data Analysis — analyzing sensor data collected from drones using tools like MATLAB, Python, or R

knowledge.what_it_is: Jenkins is an open-source automation server that enables continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines, widely adopted by QA teams to automate testing, deployment, and monitoring of software applications.
what_you_will_learn: Pipeline as Code — writing Jenkinsfile in Groovy or other languages to define CI/CD workflows
Job Configuration — creating and managing jobs, including triggers, build steps, and post-build actions
Build Steps — executing shell commands, scripts, and external tools like Maven, Gradle, and Ant within Jenkins
Parameterized Builds — using parameters to customize builds and make them more flexible
Declarative Pipelines — writing Jenkinsfiles in a declarative syntax for easier pipeline definition and maintenance
Multibranch Pipelines — automatically discovering and building branches from version control systems like Git
Docker Integration — using Docker images as build environments within Jenkins
Jenkinsfile DSL — mastering the Domain-Specific Language (DSL) used to write Jenkinsfiles

knowledge.what_it_is: GitLab CI/CD is a continuous integration and delivery tool that automates testing, building, and deployment of software applications. It is integrated with GitLab's version control system and provides features like automated testing, code review, and continuous deployment.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring .gitlab-ci.yml files to define CI/CD pipelines — specifying jobs, stages, and dependencies
Using GitLab CI variables for environment-specific configurations — predefined variables, masked variables, and encrypted variables
Implementing automated testing with GitLab CI/CD — unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests using tools like Jest, Pytest, or Cucumber
Utilizing GitLab's built-in containerization features — creating and managing Docker images, pushing to registries like Docker Hub
Deploying applications to various environments using GitLab CI/CD — production, staging, development, and testing environments
Monitoring and logging pipeline performance with GitLab CI/CD — metrics, logs, and analytics for optimization and debugging

knowledge.what_it_is: Bamboo is an open-source continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) server developed by Atlassian, used by QA teams to automate testing, deployment, and release processes.
what_you_will_learn: Bamboo agents — understanding how Bamboo executes tasks on remote machines or containers
Plans — creating workflows for automated builds, deployments, and releases
Jobs — breaking down plans into individual tasks with specific actions (e.g., compile, test, deploy)
Stages — grouping related jobs together to represent a deployment pipeline
Artifacts — managing build output files and dependencies across stages
Variables — defining and using environment variables for configuration and parameterization
Credentials — securely storing and referencing credentials for external services (e.g., Git, databases)
Bamboo API — interacting with Bamboo programmatically through RESTful APIs

knowledge.what_it_is: CircleCI is a popular Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) platform that automates software builds, tests, and deployments for various programming languages and frameworks. It provides a cloud-based infrastructure for developers to create, test, and deploy applications quickly and efficiently.
what_you_will_learn: Config files — defining CircleCI workflows using YAML or JSON files
Jobs — grouping related tasks into reusable units of work (e.g., build, test, deploy)
Steps — individual actions within a job, such as running commands or executing scripts
Commands — using shell scripts to execute custom logic in CircleCI environments
Environment variables — managing and accessing environment-specific settings
Artifacts — storing and retrieving build artifacts for later use (e.g., deployment)
Workflows — defining complex workflows with multiple jobs, dependencies, and conditions

knowledge.what_it_is: Travis CI is a popular open-source continuous integration (CI) tool used by developers to automate testing and deployment of software projects, particularly in the context of GitHub repositories. It provides a cloud-based infrastructure for running tests on various platforms, including Linux, macOS, and Windows.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring .travis.yml file — understanding how to define build environments, install dependencies, and run tests
Triggering builds manually or automatically via GitHub push events, pull requests, or cron jobs
Using environment variables for configuration, such as setting API keys or database credentials
Defining test suites and specifying test frameworks like Jest, Mocha, or Pytest
Running static analysis tools like ESLint, Prettier, or Codecov to enforce coding standards
Deploying artifacts to cloud storage services like AWS S3 or Google Cloud Storage using the deploy feature
Monitoring build logs and notifications via email, Slack, or webhooks for failed builds
Integrating with other tools like GitHub Actions, CircleCI, or GitLab CI/CD

knowledge.what_it_is: TeamCity is a commercial continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) server developed by JetBrains, widely adopted in the software industry for automating build, test, and deployment processes. It supports various programming languages, including Java, .NET, Python, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring TeamCity projects and builds — setting up VCS roots, triggers, and dependencies
Understanding build configurations and templates — creating custom build steps and scripts
Working with runners and agents — configuring agent pools, runner types, and permissions
Implementing automated testing using TeamCity's built-in test runners (e.g., JUnit, NUnit)
Using the TeamCity REST API for automation and integration with other tools (e.g., scripting, webhooks)
Configuring and managing VCS repositories in TeamCity — Git, SVN, Mercurial, etc.
Creating and scheduling builds using TeamCity's build triggers and schedules
Monitoring and analyzing build results using TeamCity's reporting and analytics features

knowledge.what_it_is: Azure DevOps Services is a suite of cloud-based tools that enables teams to plan, track, and deliver software projects from anywhere. It provides version control, continuous integration, and deployment capabilities, making it an essential tool for Quality Assurance (QA) engineers.
what_you_will_learn: Azure Pipelines — creating and managing CI/CD pipelines with YAML or graphical interface
Azure Repos — using Git repositories for source code management and collaboration
Work Items — tracking bugs, tasks, and features in a centralized database
Test Plans — authoring, executing, and reporting on test cases and test suites
Test Runs — automating test execution with Azure Pipelines or manually through the web interface
Release Management — planning, building, and deploying software releases to production environments
Azure Artifacts — publishing and consuming NuGet packages, Maven artifacts, or npm packages
Security and Compliance — configuring security settings, access controls, and compliance policies for DevOps projects
